Member-only story

9 min read
Just now

--

Every time you start building an authentication system, the same question comes up: “Should I use JWT or cookies?”
Search for the answer online and you’ll find a pile of conflicting opinions. “JWT is more modern.” “Cookies are more secure.” “Never store JWT in localStorage.” Most of these are partially true — but without the right context, they all just add to the confusion.
You can read the full story for free by clicking here
This article walks through both approaches from scratch: how they work, what the actual security trade-offs are, when each one fits, and a full Node.js implementation of both. By the end, you’ll be able to make this decision confidently for any project.
Clearing Up the Most Common Misconception
Here’s what trips most developers up: JWT is a token format, and cookies are a storage and delivery mechanism. You can store a JWT inside a cookie — and most modern secure applications actually do exactly that.
So the more accurate comparison is really between:

• Session-based authentication (cookie carries a session ID, state lives on the server)
